macro_segments.title: Rav Chilkiya's Radical Position: Shelo Lishmo as Identity-Transforming for Asham Before Its Time

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rav Chilkiya's Statement: Asham Shechuted Outside Before Its Time with Shelo Lishmo Intent
Explanation of the Chiddush: How Shelo Lishmo Changes the Identity of an Asham Before Its Time, Making It Valid
Rav Huna's Challenge: How Does Shelo Lishmo Make Something Better Rather Than Worse?

macro_segments.title: First Braita Supporting Rav Chilkiya: Olah and Asham Before Owner's Time with Shelo Lishmo

micro_segments.title: Braita's Exclusions and Inclusions: Olah Before Owner's Time (Always Kosher) vs. Asham Before Owner's Time (Debate)
Gemara's Initial Weak Reading: Attempting to Reconcile Braita with Rabbeinu Eliezer's Equation of Asham to Chatat
Clarification of the Braita's Actual Chiddush: Chatat Shelo Lishmo Before Its Time in Beit Hamikdash Still Invalid Outside
Why Shelo Lishmo Doesn't Help Chatat: Shelo Lishmo Invalidates All Sacrifices, Unlike Asham Which Transforms Identity

macro_segments.title: Second Braita and the Distinction Between Asham and Chatat Before Their Time

micro_segments.title: Braita on Olah and Chatat Lacking Time: Only Chatat Before Owner's Obligation Time Is Exempt from Shechutei Chutz
Gemara's Analysis: Chatat Exempt Only When Both Before Its Time and Shelo Lishmo; Asham Chayav in the Same Scenario
Gemara's Weak Counterargument: Trying to Eliminate the Asham-Chatat Distinction by Invoking Rabbeinu Eliezer's Equation

macro_segments.title: Rav Dimi's Transmission and the Question It Implies About Shelo Lishmo Making Asham Valid Before Its Time

micro_segments.title: Braita from Rav Levi's House: Asham Nazir and Asham Metzora Shelo Lishmon Before Owner's Time (Shelo Bizmano)
Status of These Ashamot in Beit Hamikdash: Kosher Even Shelo Lishmon, But Pasul If Before Owner's Time (Shelo Bizmano Bebealim)
Rav Dimi's Question: Apparent Contradiction Between Chayav Outside (Shelo Bizmano) and Pasul Inside (Shelo Bizmano Lishmo)
Rav Dimi's Resolution: Distinction Between Lishmo (Pasul Shelo Bizmano) and Shelo Lishmo (Kosher Shelo Bizmano)

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Parallel Challenge and Reconciliation of Mishnah with Braita on Asham Status

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi Challenges Mishnah with Braita: Mishnah Says Asham Outside Is Patur; Braita Says Chayav
Rav Ashi's Answer: Mishnah Is Lishmo (Pasul); Braita Is Shelo Lishmo (Kosher Because It Becomes Different Type)

macro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Concession: The Case Where Shelo Lishmo Actually Makes an Asham More Valid

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's Scenario: Two Ashamot Separated from the Outset, One as Backup, One Inevitably Becomes Olah
How Shelo Lishmo Works Here: Designating One as Olah Makes It Valid Before Its Time, Explaining the Chiyuv Outside
Rav Huna's Supporting Precedent: Asham Set Aside for Grazing, Slaughtered Stam, Becomes Valid as Olah

macro_segments.title: Transition to New Topic: Burning Non-Standard Materials as Violation of Shechutei Chutz

micro_segments.title: Mishnah's Rule: If You Burn Meat or Mincha That Should Be Eaten, You Are Patur from Shechutei Chutz
Braita's Enumeration: Items That Should Be Eaten (Chatat Meat, Asham Meat, Kodshei Kodashim, Shelamim) Are Not Liable for Burning

macro_segments.title: Derivation of the Burning Exemption and Definition of What Activities Are Included in Shechutei Chutz

micro_segments.title: Pasuk-Based Derivation: Only Things Whose Purpose Is Burning (Like Olah) Are Liable; Things With Earlier Finishing Stages Are Exempt
Enumeration of Exempt Preparatory Acts: Yotzek, Bolel, Potez, Molach, Menif, Magish, Mesader Shulchan, and Others Are All Patur
Principle of Chiddush: Only Activities That Conclude the Process (Like Zrikas Hadam or Nisuch) Are Chayav; Middle Stages Are Patur

macro_segments.title: History of Who Performed the Avoda: Bechor (Firstborn) Before the Mishkan Was Erected

micro_segments.title: Mishnah's Claim: Until the Mishkan Was Erected, It Was the Bechor Who Performed the Avoda
Rav Chisda's Objection: Rav Assi Said the Bechor Brought Sacrifices Only at Har Sinai, Then Stopped (Before Mishkan Erected)
Rashi's Alternative Reading: Rav Huna Was Merely Fetching Animals; Kohanim Were Doing the Avoda Even at Har Sinai

macro_segments.title: Braita Supporting Mishnah: Bechor and Bamot Permitted Until Mishkan; Flexible Animals and Blemished Ones Allowed

micro_segments.title: Braita's Statement: Before Mishkan Erected, Bamot Were Permitted, Avoda Was With Bechor, All Kosher Animals Acceptable
Braita's Details: Animals Could Be Male or Female, With or Without Blemish; All Brought as Olah
Braita's Final Point: Only One Detail of Olah Applied Even Before Mishkan: Hafshet V'nituach (Flaying and Chopping)

macro_segments.title: Debate Over When Kohanim Status Began: At Har Sinai or Only at Mishkan; Reconciliation Through Tannai Argument

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua ben Korcha: Directive to Keep Distance at Har Sinai Was to Bechor (Supporting Mishnah's Bechor-Until-Mishkan View)
Rabbi (Judah HaNasi): Directive to Keep Distance Was to Nadav and Avihu, Implying Kohanim Status Began at Har Sinai
Support for Kohanim Interpretation: Pasuk on Sanctification (Bikrovai Ekadesh) Points to Nadav and Avihu as the Initial Kehuna

macro_segments.title: The Story of Nadav and Avihu: Divine Judgment and Aharon's Acceptance in Silence as Model for Faith

micro_segments.title: God's Warning at Har Sinai: Those Close to God Must Keep Their Distance or Face Divine Wrath (Pen Yifrotz Bam)
Moshe's Consolation to Aharon: God's Sanctification Through Nadav and Avihu's Death Reveals Divine Glory to All Israel
Aharon's Response: He Fell Silent (Shatak), Accepting God's Judgment Without Full Understanding; Received Reward for His Silence
Talmudic Principle: Ecclesiastes and Psalms on Silence: Sometimes Silence in the Face of Divine Judgment Is the Appropriate Response
Rabbi Chiya bar Abba's Interpretation: God Is Glorified When He Executes Judgment Against the Holy; Awesome from His Sanctified Ones

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Debate on When Detailed Laws Applied: Rabbi Ishmael (Principles at Sinai, Details at Ohel Moed) vs. Rabbi Akiva (All at Sinai)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Ishmael's Position: General Principles (Like Offering Sacrifices) at Har Sinai; Specific Details (Like Hafshet V'nituach) at Ohel Moed
Rabbi Akiva's Position: Both Principles and Details Were Stated at Har Sinai; Repeated at Ohel Moed and Arvot Moav
Apparent Contradiction: Why Would Flaying-and-Chopping Detail Apply Before Mishkan If Other Olah Details (Male, Blemishless) Applied Only Later?
Unresolved Question About Animal Requirements: How Do We Know Before Mishkan All Animals, Male and Female, Blemished and Perfect, Were Acceptable?

topical_tags.term: shelo lishmo (not for its designated name)
Rav Ashi (parallel challenge to Rav Dimi's approach)
Rabbi Eliezer (equation of asham to chatat)
raui l'pesach ohel moed (fit for entry into Ohel Moed)
Rabbi Ishmael (klalot at Sinai, pratim at Moed)
